elements.sent-2411-001: We establish a family of inequalities that hold true on any $6$ points in any $\mathrm{CAT (0)$ space.}
elements.sent-2411-002: We prove that the validity of these inequalities does not follow from any properties of $5$-point subsets of $\mathrm{CAT (0)$ spaces.}
elements.sent-2411-003: In particular, the validity of these inequalities does not follow from the $\mathrm{CAT (0)$ $4$-point condition.}
elements.sent-2411-004: Many simple necessary and sufficient conditions for a geodesic metric space to be a $\mathrm{CAT (0)$ space have been known.}
elements.sent-2411-005: However, to find a characterization of those metric spaces that admit a distance-preserving embedding into a $\mathrm{CAT (0)$ space is an open problem posed by Gromov (see \cite[Section 1.19+]{Gr1 , \cite[\S15]{Gr2 , \cite[Section 7]{AKP , \cite[Chapter 10, E]{AKP-book , \cite[Section 1.4]{ANN , \cite[Section 2.2]{Bac and \cite[Section 1]{toyoda-five ).}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-006: A condition for points $x_1 ,\ldots ,x_n$ in a metric space $(X,d_X )$ is called a {\em quadratic metric inequality on $n$ points if it can be written in the form \begin{equation}\label{qm-ineq} 0\leq \sum_{i=1}^n\sum_{j=1}^n a_{ij}d_X (x_i ,x_j)^2\end{equation} by a real symmetric $n\times n$ matrix $(a_{ij )$ with $a_{kk =0$ for any $k\in\{ 1,\ldots ,n\}$ (see \cite[Section 1.4.1]{ANN ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-007: If \eqref{qm-ineq holds true for all $x_1 ,\ldots ,x_n \in X$, then we say that the metric space $X$ {\em satisfies the quadratic metric inequality \eqref{qm-ineq .}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: We call a quadratic metric inequality a {\em $\mathrm{CAT (0)$ quadratic metric inequality if every $\mathrm{CAT (0)$ space satisfies it.}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Andoni, Naor and Neiman \cite[Proposition~3]{ANN proved that an $n$-point metric space admits a distance-preserving embedding into a $\mathrm{CAT (0)$ space if and only if it satisfies all $\mathrm{CAT (0)$ quadratic metric inequalities on $n$ points.}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: This result tells us that we can characterize those metric spaces that admit a distance-preserving embedding into a $\mathrm{CAT (0)$ space if we can characterize $\mathrm{CAT (0)$ quadratic metric inequalities.}}
elements.sent-2411-011: We recall the following family of $\mathrm{CAT (0)$ quadratic metric inequalities on $4$ points.}
elements.sent-2411-012: We say that a metric space $(X,d_X )$ satisfies the {\em $\boxtimes$-inequalities if we have \begin{equation}0 \leq (1-s)(1-t) d_X (x,y)^2 +s(1-t) d_X (y,z)^2 +st d_X (z,w)^2 +(1-s)t d_X (w,x)^2 -s(1-s) d_X (x,z)^2 -t(1-t) d_X (y,w)^2\end{equation} for any $s, t \in\lbrack0,1\rbrack$ and any $x,y,z,w\in X$.}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}0 \leq (1-s)(1-t) d_X (x,y)^2 +s(1-t) d_X (y,z)^2 +st d_X (z,w)^2 +(1-s)t d_X (w,x)^2 -s(1-s) d_X (x,z)^2 -t(1-t) d_X (y,w)^2\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-013: Gromov \cite{Gr2 and Sturm \cite{St proved independently that every $\mathrm{CAT (0)$ space satisfies the $\boxtimes$-inequalities.}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: The name ``$\boxtimes$-inequalities" is based on a notation used by Gromov \cite{Gr2 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Sturm \cite{St called these inequalities the {\em weighted quadruple inequalities .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: The present author \cite{toyoda-five proved that if a metric space $X$ satisfies the $\boxtimes$-inequalities, then $X$ satisfies all $\mathrm{CAT (0)$ quadratic metric inequalities on $5$ points, which implies the following theorem.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: On the other hand, Nina Lebedeva constructed a family of $6$-point metric spaces that satisfy the $\boxtimes$-inequalities but do not admit a distance-preserving embedding into any $\mathrm{CAT (0)$ space (see \cite[Section 7.2]{AKP ).}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Therefore, it follows from the result of Andoni, Naor and Neiman that there exist $\mathrm{CAT (0)$ quadratic metric inequalities on $6$ points that do not follow from the $\boxtimes$-inequalities.}
elements.sent-2411-019: However, to the best of my knowledge, no such $\mathrm{CAT (0)$ quadratic metric inequality has ever been known explicitly.}
elements.sent-2411-020: In this paper, we establish the first explicit examples of such inequalities.
elements.sent-2411-021: The following theorem is our main result.

elements.sent-2411-065: A {\em geodesic in a metric space $X$ is a distance-preserving embedding of an interval of the real line into $X$.}
elements.sent-2411-066: For any $x,y\in X$, the image of a geodesic $\gamma :\lbrack 0,d_X (x,y)\rbrack\to X$ with $\gamma (0)=x$ and $\gamma (d_{X (x,y))=y$ is called a {\em geodesic segment with endpoints $x$ and $y$}.}
elements.sent-2411-067: A metric space $X$ is called {\em geodesic if for any $x,y\in X$, there exists a geodesic segment with endpoints $x$ and $y$.}
elements.sent-2411-068: We recall the definition of $\mathrm{CAT (0)$ spaces.}
elements.sent-2411-069: A complete geodesic metric space $(X, d_X )$ is called a {\em $\mathrm{CAT (0)$ space if for any $x,y\in X$, and any geodesic $\gamma : [0,d_X (x,y)] \to X$ with $\gamma(0) = x$ and $\gamma(d_X (x,y)) = y$, the inequality \begin{equation}\label{CAT(0)-def-ineq} d_X \left(z, \gamma(t d_X (x,y) )\right)^2 \leq (1-t)d_X (z,x) ^2 +td_X (z,y)^2 -(1-t)t d_X (x,y)^2\end{equation} holds true for any $z\in X$ and any $t\in\lbrack 0,1\rbrack$.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: In a $\mathrm{CAT (0)$ space $X$, for any $x,y\in X$, a geodesic segment with endpoints $x$ and $y$ exists uniquely.}
elements.sent-2411-071: It is straightforward to see that \eqref{CAT(0)-def-ineq holds with equality when $X$ is a Hilbert space.}
elements.sent-2411-073: Let $(X,d_X )$ be a $\mathrm{CAT (0)$ space, and let $x,y,z\in X$.}

elements.sent-2411-140: A metric space $(X,d_X )$ is said to satisfy the {\em $\mathrm{Cycl _{n (0)$ condition if for any map $f\colon V\to X$, there exists a map $g\colon V\to\mathcal{H}$ to a Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$ such that \begin{equation}\begin{cases} \|g(u)-g(v)\|\leq d_X (f(u),f(v)),\quadif \{ u,v\}\in E, \|g(u)-g(v)\|\geq d_X (f(u),f(v)),\quadif \{ u,v\}\not\in E \end{cases}\end{equation} for any $u,v\in V$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-141: A metric space $(X,d_X )$ is said to satisfy the {\em $G$-comparison if for any map $f\colon V\to X$, there exists a map $g\colon V\to\mathcal{H}$ to a Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$ such that \begin{equation}\begin{cases} \|g(u)-g(v)\|\leq d_X (f(u),f(v)),\quadif \{ u,v\}\in E, \|g(u)-g(v)\|\geq d_X (f(u),f(v)),\quadif \{ u,v\}\not\in E \end{cases}\end{equation} for any $u,v\in V$.}

elements.sent-2411-143: For any $x,y\in X$, the image of a geodesic $\gamma\colon \lbrack 0,d_X (x,y)\rbrack\to X$ with $\gamma (0)=x$ and $\gamma (d_{X (x,y))=y$ is called a {\em geodesic segment with endpoints $x$ and $y$}.}
elements.sent-2411-144: A complete geodesic metric space $(X, d_X )$ is called a {\em $\mathrm{CAT (0)$ space if for any $x,y\in X$, and any geodesic $\gamma\colon\lbrack 0,d_X (x,y)\rbrack\to X$ with $\gamma(0) = x$ and $\gamma(d_X (x,y)) = y$, the inequality \begin{equation}\label{CAT(0)-def-ineq} d_X \left(z, \gamma(t d_X (x,y) )\right)^2 \leq (1-t)d_X (z,x) ^2 +td_X (z,y)^2 -(1-t)t d_X (x,y)^2\end{equation} holds true for any $z\in X$ and any $t\in\lbrack 0,1\rbrack$.}}

onumber +s(1-t)t(1-a)(1-b) \, d_{X}(x_0 ,y_0 )^2 +s(1-t)ta(1-b) \, d_{X}(x_1 ,y_0 )^2 
onumber +(1-t)t(s-a)b \, d_{X}(x_0 ,y_1 )^2 , \label{6-point-deseired-ineq}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-157: Furthermore, we assume that $X$ is the three dimensional Euclidean space $\mathbb{R ^3$, and the points $x_0 ,x_1 ,y_0 ,y_1 ,z_0 ,z_1 \in X$ satisfy \eqref{lebedeva-condition and \begin{equation}(1-t)y_1 +t\left( (1-s) x_{0}+s x_{1}\right) = (1-c)z_{0}+c z_{1},\quad (1-a)x_{0}+a x_{1} = (1-b) y_{0}+b y_{1}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-040: \begin{equation}(1-t)y_1 +t\left( (1-s) x_{0}+s x_{1}\right) = (1-c)z_{0}+c z_{1},\quad (1-a)x_{0}+a x_{1} = (1-b) y_{0}+b y_{1}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-158: Let $L=\{ x_0 ,x_1 ,y_0 ,y_1 ,z_0 ,z_1 \}$, and define $d_{\varepsilon}\colon L\times L\to\lbrack 0,\infty )$ by \eqref{L-distance-def for each $\varepsilon\in\lbrack 0,\infty )$.}
